1: <header>Carbon footprint of conventional and organic beef production systems: An Italian case study </header>
C. Buratti a,* , F. Fantozzi a M. Barbanera a , E. Lascaro a , M. Chiorri b, L. Cecchini b

<list>a CRB, Biomass Research Centre, Via G. Duranti, 63, 06125 Perugia, Italy 
b Department of Agricultural, Food and Environmental Sciences, University of Perugia, Borgo XX Giugno, 74, 06121 Perugia, Italy</list>
2:
<list>Organic and conventional beef produc- tion are compared by carbon footprint. 
Conventional system has lower GHG emissions. 
The most impacting phase is represent- ed by enteric fermentation in both sce- narios. 
Sensitivity analysis evaluates the contri- bution from changes in soil C.</list>
4: Article history: Received 19 May 2016 Received in revised form 27 September 2016 Accepted 10 October 2016 Available online 23 October 2016
Keywords: Livestock Meat production GHG emissions Carbon footprint Life Cycle Assessment
5: <header>A B S T R A C T </header>

Beef cattle production is a widespread activity in Italy in the agricultural field and determines an important im- pact on environment and resources consumption. Carbon footprint evaluation is thus necessary to evaluate the contributions of the different stages and the possible improvements of the production chain. In this study, two typical Italian beef production systems, a conventional and an organic one are investigated in order to evaluate the greenhouse gas emissions from "cradle to gate farm" by a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) ap- proach; the carbon footprint (CF) per 1 kg of live weight meat is calculated. The contributions from feed production, enteric fermentation, and manure management are taken into account, in order to compare the life cycle of the two productions; also the carbon balance in soil is evaluated, in order to verify the impact in a life cycle perspective. The results of CF calculation of the two farms show that organic system (24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight) produce more GHG emissions than the conventional one (18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight) and that the enteric fermenta- tion is the more heavy contribution, with a range of 50-54% of the global CF value. Improvements of the produc- tion chain could be realized by accurate feeding strategies, in order to obtain reduction of methane emissions from enteric digestion of cattles.

7: Worldwide the agriculture sector, including crop and livestock pro- duction, forestry and associated land use changes, was estimated to con- tribute up to 30% of the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. According to Tubiello et al. (2013), at the global level, the largest source of GHG emis- sions within agriculture is enteric fermentation, which accounted in 2012 for 38.6% of the sector's total GHG outputs.

The livestock sector is responsible for about 7.1 CO2eq/yr (14.5% of all anthropogenic emissions) and, in particular, the cattle sector emits about 4.3 million tons of (65% of the livestock sector) of which about 2.8 million tons are associated with beef production (Opio et al., 2013).

In Italy the agricultural sector represents the second largest source of GHG emissions (6.9% of total anthropogenic emissions in 2011). In 2012, the livestock farming, mainly ruminants, was the principal con- tributor, above all due to the enteric fermentation (37.5% of GHG emis- sions from the agriculture sector) and manure management (20%).

The beef production industry is particularly relevant for the Italian agribusiness. Italy is the fourth European producer of beef (Eurostat, 2013), with three main production systems: dairy breed bull calves fat- tened for meat production (805,000 heads slaughtered in 2012, corre- sponding to 12% of the Italian cattle meat production), dairy culled COWS (507,000 heads slaughtered in 2012, representing 15% of the Ital- ian cattle meat production), and beef cattle breeds (2,039,000 heads slaughtered in 2012, representing 69% of the Italian cattle meat produc- tion) (Rama, 2014).

Beef cattle production is largely based on the fattening of Italian young cattle (62%), while the other fraction (38%) derives from finishing of imported young bulls and heifers (mainly from France, Ireland, and eastern European countries). With reference to the breeds of beef farms, Italy is characterized by a great number of autochthon breeds (Piemontese, Podolica, Chianina, Marchigiana, Maremmana, etc.), spreading over specific regions. In Umbria region cattle livestock farms for meat production are 2730, with 35,265 heads in 2014. The most typ- ical cattle breed for beef breed farming systems is Chianina (18,772 heads corresponding to about 80% of the beef heads) (Umbria Region, 2014), while the diffusion of other valuable beef breeds is much more limited: Limousine (3391 heads), Charolais (1209), Marchigiana (1129). Umbria region is also the second largest Italian area for diffusion of Chianina breed after Tuscany.

From the production system point of view, in Europe the organic cat- tle livestock for meat production is still not widespread, because the share of the organic sector in relation with the whole bovine sector rep- resents only around 3% in the EU-15 (EC, 2013). However, the number of certified organic cattle heads registered a significant growth from 2005 (1,490,201 heads) to 2012 (3,250,557 heads) in the EU-27. In Italy the organic beef cattle represents about 4% of the bovine sector and Central Italy is the area characterized by the wider diffusion of the organic cattle livestock production for beef cattle (INEA, 2014). The main barriers to the spreading of organic cattle breeding are the con- straints imposed by European (EC, 2007; European Union, 2008) and National (Decree of the Ministry of Agricultural, Food and Forestry Policies No. 18,354/09) regulations, requiring that:

<list>cattle have to be fed with milk until at least 3 months of age; 
cattle housing systems should be mostly based on the use of grazing, when available; 
a minimum of 60% of the dry matter intake of the beef cattle should be made by forage; 
the use of chemical fertilizers, pesticides, antibiotics, feed additives, growth hormones, and genetically engineered breeding inputs has to be avoided.</list>
In order to evaluate the environmental profile intensity of beef pro- duction, it is important to adopt a whole system modeling approach.

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology (ISO, 2006a, 2006b) allows to identify and analyze the environmental impacts and hotspots of products and service systems (Rinaldi et al., 2014; Buratti et al., 2015) and should be the basis of any decision making strategy for environ- mental improvements in a life cycle perspective (Gonzalez-García et al., 2009). However, the comprehensiveness of the LCA methodology, due to the inclusion of a wide spectrum of environmental indicators, presents weaknesses when results are communicated to stakeholders and the general public (Weidema et al., 2008). Thus, the application of a single issue indicator, such as carbon footprint (CF), has become in- creasingly popular (Rugani et al., 2013). In particular, CF allows to eval- uate the impact of a product in terms of global warming, providing an assessment of GHG emissions during part or all of its life (BSI, 2011).

The use of CF for assessing the environmental impact of beef produc- tion systems could have several limitations, mainly due to the trade-offs with other relevant environmental variables (Picasso et al., 2014). How- ever, CF is strongly linked to energy consumption and therefore it repre- sents other underlying environmental impacts (Weidema et al., 2008).

In order to overcome the differences in methodological approaches between different studies, causing an unfair comparison of calculated CFs (Flysjö et al., 2011), ISO 14067 was introduced with the aim of pro- viding principles, requirements, and guidelines for the quantification and communication of the carbon footprint of a product (ISO/TS 14067, 2014).

LCA method has been widely used to evaluate the environmental performance of beef production, comparing different origin of calves (dairy or suckler calves), production systems (organic or conventional), and different types of diet (concentrate- roughage-based systems) (de Vries et al., 2015). Furthermore, the existing studies are referred to specific countries such as France, Ireland, Japan, United States, Thai- land, Brazil mostly based on farm model data (Ogino et al., 2016). In par- ticular, only three studies concern the comparison of the organic and conventional production systems, and all of these are referred to middle and northern European countries (de Vries et al., 2015); the results show that the best scenario is not uniquely defined because the relative difference between organic and conventional production, in terms of GHG emissions per unit of product, ranges from - 15% to 15%.

Therefore, further research is needed in order to understand if the organic system can be linked with lower environmental impact, also in relation to other geographical areas. The aim of this study was to eval- uate and to compare the GHG emissions of organic and conventional beef production systems in Italy, using actual data from two typical farms located in the Umbria region.

In order to evaluate the environmental profile intensity of beef pro- duction, it is important to adopt a whole system modeling approach.

Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) methodology (ISO, 2006a, 2006b) allows to identify and analyze the environmental impacts and hotspots of products and service systems (Rinaldi et al., 2014; Buratti et al., 2015) and should be the basis of any decision making strategy for environ- mental improvements in a life cycle perspective (Gonzalez-García et al., 2009). However, the comprehensiveness of the LCA methodology, due to the inclusion of a wide spectrum of environmental indicators, presents weaknesses when results are communicated to stakeholders and the general public (Weidema et al., 2008). Thus, the application of a single issue indicator, such as carbon footprint (CF), has become in- creasingly popular (Rugani et al., 2013). In particular, CF allows to eval- uate the impact of a product in terms of global warming, providing an assessment of GHG emissions during part or all of its life (BSI, 2011).

The use of CF for assessing the environmental impact of beef produc- tion systems could have several limitations, mainly due to the trade-offs with other relevant environmental variables (Picasso et al., 2014). How- ever, CF is strongly linked to energy consumption and therefore it repre- sents other underlying environmental impacts (Weidema et al., 2008).

In order to overcome the differences in methodological approaches between different studies, causing an unfair comparison of calculated CFs (Flysjö et al., 2011), ISO 14067 was introduced with the aim of pro- viding principles, requirements, and guidelines for the quantification and communication of the carbon footprint of a product (ISO/TS 14067, 2014).

LCA method has been widely used to evaluate the environmental performance of beef production, comparing different origin of calves (dairy or suckler calves), production systems (organic or conventional), and different types of diet (concentrate- roughage-based systems) (de Vries et al., 2015). Furthermore, the existing studies are referred to specific countries such as France, Ireland, Japan, United States, Thai- land, Brazil mostly based on farm model data (Ogino et al., 2016). In par- ticular, only three studies concern the comparison of the organic and conventional production systems, and all of these are referred to middle and northern European countries (de Vries et al., 2015); the results show that the best scenario is not uniquely defined because the relative difference between organic and conventional production, in terms of GHG emissions per unit of product, ranges from - 15% to 15%.

Therefore, further research is needed in order to understand if the organic system can be linked with lower environmental impact, also in relation to other geographical areas. The aim of this study was to eval- uate and to compare the GHG emissions of organic and conventional beef production systems in Italy, using actual data from two typical farms located in the Umbria region.
12: Worldwide the agriculture sector, including crop and livestock pro- duction, forestry and associated land use changes, was estimated to con- tribute up to 30% of the greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions. According to Tubiello et al. (2013), at the global level, the largest source of GHG emis- sions within agriculture is enteric fermentation, which accounted in 2012 for 38.6% of the sector's total GHG outputs.

The livestock sector is responsible for about 7.1 CO2eq/yr (14.5% of all anthropogenic emissions) and, in particular, the cattle sector emits about 4.3 million tons of (65% of the livestock sector) of which about 2.8 million tons are associated with beef production (Opio et al., 2013).

In Italy the agricultural sector represents the second largest source of GHG emissions (6.9% of total anthropogenic emissions in 2011). In 2012, the livestock farming, mainly ruminants, was the principal con- tributor, above all due to the enteric fermentation (37.5% of GHG emis- sions from the agriculture sector) and manure management (20%).

The beef production industry is particularly relevant for the Italian agribusiness. Italy is the fourth European producer of beef (Eurostat, 2013), with three main production systems: dairy breed bull calves fat- tened for meat production (805,000 heads slaughtered in 2012, corre- sponding to 12% of the Italian cattle meat production), dairy culled COWS (507,000 heads slaughtered in 2012, representing 15% of the Ital- ian cattle meat production), and beef cattle breeds (2,039,000 heads slaughtered in 2012, representing 69% of the Italian cattle meat produc- tion) (Rama, 2014).

Beef cattle production is largely based on the fattening of Italian young cattle (62%), while the other fraction (38%) derives from finishing of imported young bulls and heifers (mainly from France, Ireland, and eastern European countries). With reference to the breeds of beef farms, Italy is characterized by a great number of autochthon breeds (Piemontese, Podolica, Chianina, Marchigiana, Maremmana, etc.), spreading over specific regions. In Umbria region cattle livestock farms for meat production are 2730, with 35,265 heads in 2014. The most typ- ical cattle breed for beef breed farming systems is Chianina (18,772 heads corresponding to about 80% of the beef heads) (Umbria Region, 2014), while the diffusion of other valuable beef breeds is much more limited: Limousine (3391 heads), Charolais (1209), Marchigiana (1129). Umbria region is also the second largest Italian area for diffusion of Chianina breed after Tuscany.

From the production system point of view, in Europe the organic cat- tle livestock for meat production is still not widespread, because the share of the organic sector in relation with the whole bovine sector rep- resents only around 3% in the EU-15 (EC, 2013). However, the number of certified organic cattle heads registered a significant growth from 2005 (1,490,201 heads) to 2012 (3,250,557 heads) in the EU-27. In Italy the organic beef cattle represents about 4% of the bovine sector and Central Italy is the area characterized by the wider diffusion of the organic cattle livestock production for beef cattle (INEA, 2014). The main barriers to the spreading of organic cattle breeding are the con- straints imposed by European (EC, 2007; European Union, 2008) and National (Decree of the Ministry of Agricultural, Food and Forestry Policies No. 18,354/09) regulations, requiring that:

<list>cattle have to be fed with milk until at least 3 months of age; 
cattle housing systems should be mostly based on the use of grazing, when available; 
a minimum of 60% of the dry matter intake of the beef cattle should be made by forage; 
the use of chemical fertilizers, pesticides, antibiotics, feed additives, growth hormones, and genetically engineered breeding inputs has to be avoided.</list>

15: The first step of LCA is the definition of the goal and scope of the study; in this case, the goal was the evaluation and the comparison of the environmental impact, in terms of carbon footprint, of two Chianina beef production systems: a conventional system (CON) and an organic system (ORG). For each system cow-calf farms, characterized by self- produced cattle, were considered, because cow-calf system is the most typical one in Central and South Italy (Rama, 2014).

Two case study farms were selected in order to obtain primary data on the basis of the following criteria: (1) representativeness of Chianina beef farms operating in Umbria, in terms of herd size, farm size, and housing system; and (2) implementation of an accounting and manage- ment system organized to provide comprehensive and good-quality data for the LCA analysis.

In particular, typical farming system in Umbria was identified on the basis of data reported by BDN (2016) and ANABIC (2014), showing that the animals are mainly reared in an extensive production way based on a loose housing system on deep straw bedding with an outdoor paddock.
The two investigated farms that represent CON and ORG systems are located in Perugia Province. The annual mean temperature and annual
Table 1 Annual input and output of the two beef production systems.

average precipitation are 13.7 °C and 804.5 mm/yr, respectively. Table 1 provides basic production data of the CON and ORG farms. The CON farm has a herd of 223 animals (including calves, bullock, heifers, cows, and bulls) while the herd of the ORG farm is composed of 208 animals.

In the CON farm, calving occurs during spring and each cow weans 0.9 calves per year, of which 0.2 heifer calves per year is used for re- placement. In this system, age at first calving is 30 months and age for slaughtering is 20 months for bullock and heifers and 17 and 8 years for cull cows and bulls, respectively. The diet for each animal category is mainly composed by hay produced on the farm and cereal meals. In the ORG farm, except for small amounts of sunflower meal from semi-dehulled seeds and mineral salts, the animal feed is produced at the farm itself. Daily gains for each group of animal are estimated from data on age and weight at slaughter. About 0.2 heifer calves per year is used for replacement and 0.85 calf is weaned per cow per year. In the ORG farm, the age at first calving is 34 months; the age at slaugh- ter is 24 months for bullock and heifers, 13 years for cull cows and 15 years for bulls. The feed is rich in corn silage for cows and bulls and in corn meal for bullock and heifers.

19: The first step of LCA is the definition of the goal and scope of the study; in this case, the goal was the evaluation and the comparison of the environmental impact, in terms of carbon footprint, of two Chianina beef production systems: a conventional system (CON) and an organic system (ORG). For each system cow-calf farms, characterized by self- produced cattle, were considered, because cow-calf system is the most typical one in Central and South Italy (Rama, 2014).

Two case study farms were selected in order to obtain primary data on the basis of the following criteria: (1) representativeness of Chianina beef farms operating in Umbria, in terms of herd size, farm size, and housing system; and (2) implementation of an accounting and manage- ment system organized to provide comprehensive and good-quality data for the LCA analysis.

In particular, typical farming system in Umbria was identified on the basis of data reported by BDN (2016) and ANABIC (2014), showing that the animals are mainly reared in an extensive production way based on a loose housing system on deep straw bedding with an outdoor paddock.
The two investigated farms that represent CON and ORG systems are located in Perugia Province. The annual mean temperature and annual
Table 1 Annual input and output of the two beef production systems.

average precipitation are 13.7 °C and 804.5 mm/yr, respectively. Table 1 provides basic production data of the CON and ORG farms. The CON farm has a herd of 223 animals (including calves, bullock, heifers, cows, and bulls) while the herd of the ORG farm is composed of 208 animals.

In the CON farm, calving occurs during spring and each cow weans 0.9 calves per year, of which 0.2 heifer calves per year is used for re- placement. In this system, age at first calving is 30 months and age for slaughtering is 20 months for bullock and heifers and 17 and 8 years for cull cows and bulls, respectively. The diet for each animal category is mainly composed by hay produced on the farm and cereal meals. In the ORG farm, except for small amounts of sunflower meal from semi-dehulled seeds and mineral salts, the animal feed is produced at the farm itself. Daily gains for each group of animal are estimated from data on age and weight at slaughter. About 0.2 heifer calves per year is used for replacement and 0.85 calf is weaned per cow per year. In the ORG farm, the age at first calving is 34 months; the age at slaugh- ter is 24 months for bullock and heifers, 13 years for cull cows and 15 years for bulls. The feed is rich in corn silage for cows and bulls and in corn meal for bullock and heifers.

21: The functional unit of the study was defined as the production of 1 kg of live weight meat from bullocks and heifers (HM) at the farm gate level. Besides the main product, low quality meat is produced from COWS and bulls (LM) that are culled when they are too old to con- tinue production or have other problems. Therefore, GHG emissions were splitted from the beef production system between the two prod- ucts. ISO 14040 (2006) recommends using system expansion, whenever possible, instead of allocation. The expansion of the system boundaries requires the evaluation of a different way to obtain the same product, but, in this case, it was not possible to identify an alternative product system for the co-product. Thus, the economic allocation was preferred to other allocation criteria indicated by ISO prescriptions (e.g. physical allocation), considering that it is commonly used in food product-relat- ed studies (González-García et al., 2013) and it is in compliance with the guidelines of the Product Category Rules (PCR) for meat of mammals of the International EPD System (Environdec, 2012). Value of the HM was set to 4.2 EUR per kg live weight, while the value of the LM was assumed as 1.5 EUR per kg live weight, basing on the budgets of the analyzed farms. According to the Italian Institute of Services for Agriculture and Food Market (ISMEA) price database, the value of LM is aligned with current market trend, while the value of the HM is quite higher than
the average Italian price (3.2 EUR per kg live weight), showing that the market prices depend on the local supply-demand situation.

Emissions were also allocated between each feed item and associat- ed co-products using the economic approach. The allocation factors were drawn from Environdec (2014): 6.9% for organic wheat straw, 7.5% for conventional wheat straw, and 8.7% for organic barley straw.

Both farms produced excess manure with respect to crop require- ments, giving this amount to some neighboring farms. Because no eco- nomic value was attributed to the surplus manure, system expansion was employed with the exported manure. Thus, the beef production system will be credited for the manure N content that replaces the use of synthetic fertilizers. In particular the N content of the manure, obtain- ed from chemical analysis performed by the companies, was equal to 0.6% and 0.3% for CON and ORG farms, respectively; the GHG emissions associated with the production of N in synthetic fertilizer were taken from Ecoinvent v3.0 database.

23: The system boundaries include the cradle-to-farm-gate system, con- sidering all of the processes upstream of farm production up to the product leave the farm gate (Fig. 1). Therefore, the study takes into ac- count both direct impacts due to on-farm production processes and the indirect impacts related to the manufacture and transport of syn- thetic fertilizers, diesel, pesticides, seeds, off-farm feeds production (sunflower meal from semi-dehulled seeds, wheat straw and mineral salts), and plastic.

As seen in the Fig. 1, the beef production system includes the cow- calf stage, considering the impacts for mother cow growing and man- agement, and fattening stage from the birth to the age of slaughtering of bullocks and heifers.

Emissions deriving from the construction of equipments and farm facilities were not included. Emissions from direct land use change were excluded from the analysis because the surface invested for animal feed production was already a cropland. CO2 from livestock respiration was not taken into account because it is not considered a net source under the Kyoto Protocol. Soil carbon changes were not included in GHG estimations because of a lack of site-specific data. However, a sen- sitivity analysis to the carbon sequestration by soils was carried out, as described in detail in Section 3.4.

25: Data collection for the evaluation of the carbon footprint of the two Italian beef production systems was mostly based on the site investiga- tions during two seasons (2013/2014 and 2014/2015). This short period of observation was considered sufficiently representative since manag- er of each farm confirmed little variability of the management practices. For both models the data were integrated with Literature studies and LCA software database.
26: Fodder was mainly produced inside the farm system and only semi- dehulled sunflower meal and salts were assumed to be purchased close to the beef herd both for ORG and CON farms. Inventory data for the ex- ternal feeds production were derived from Ecoinvent v3.0 database; diesel for the transport distance of 50 km by truck from the manufactur- er to the farm was also included. Wastage of purchased feed was not di- rectly accounted. For the homegrown feeds primary data were collected from the analyzed farms through a field survey with the help of a struc- tured questionnaire. Annual input and output for growing 1 ha of the fodder crops are shown in Tables 2 and 3. The yield values are in the same order of magnitude that the ones reported by ISTAT (2016) for the Italian territory, as well as the N-fertilizer input data were in line with those suggested by the integrated production guidelines of Umbria (Umbria Region, 2016).

Upstream GHG emissions associated to the production and transport of agricultural inputs were accounted for using emission factors for fer- tilizers and pesticides, and mineral salts taken from Ecoinvent v3.0 data- base. CON farm synthetic fertilizers were also employed and 53% of the ma- nure was disposed of on the croplands of the nearby farms (average transport distance of 20 km). No irrigation was used in the farms, while both farms employed electricity to grind cereal grains. Italian spe- cific GHG emission factor (0.337 kg CO2eq/kWh) was used to account for electricity supplied to farms (ISPRA, 2015), which was considered more reliable than the 0.216 kg CO2eq/kWh from the Ecoinvent v3.0 database.

The IPCC (2006)-Tier 2 approach was used to calculate both direct and indirect emissions when nitrogen based synthetic or organic fertil- izers are applied to the soil. Direct nitrous oxide emission is about 1% of the total nitrogen applied to the soil. The volatile fraction of fertilizers (ammonia and nitrogen oxides) was assumed to be 10% for synthetic fertilizers and 20% for organic fertilizers (IPCC, 2013). The emission fac- tor of 1% was used to account for indirect N20 emissions from atmo- spheric deposition. The fraction of nitrogen added to soils which is lost through leaching and runoff was assumed to be 30% (IPCC, 2013) and 0.75% of this amount was considered as having oxidized to N-N20. Urea applied as a fertilizer also emits CO2: 0.2 kg/kg of the applied urea is carbon by weight, that will eventually oxidize to CO2.

36: Methane emissions arising from enteric fermentation were estimat- ed according to IPCC Tier 2 approach (IPCC, 2006), based on gross ener- gy requirements and digestible energy of feed. The gross energy (GE) intake was calculated using feed composition tables of all ingredients (Feedpedia, 2015). Then CH4 emissions were determined as a percent- age of the GE intake considering the CH4 conversion factors (Ym) for each diet. Ym values were estimated taking into account the digestible energy (DE, MJ/kg of dry matter) in feeds using the following equation (Gerber et al., 2010):
37: The IPCC Tier 2 approach was also employed for the evaluation of CH4 emissions from manure management, based on the volatile solid production by the cows, the CH4 conversion factor (MCF) for the ma- nure management practice, and the maximum possible CH4 production rate from the volatile solids in the manure. In particular the excreted volatile solids were calculated based on feed intake rate, digestibility, and dry matter content. In both farms solid stable manure is stockpiled over the year and then used on agricultural farm land to meet the crop N requirements. If the amount of manure exceeds these values, it was con- sidered that this excess is spread on the fields of neighboring farms, neglecting the emissions from its transport. For direct N20 emissions from manure management, IPCC Tier 2 algorithm was used based on the amount of nitrogen excreted (Nex) as faeces and urine, and the ma- nure management system. The amount of nitrogen excreted was deter- mined based on the crude protein intake through feed and the protein required for growth. The crude protein intake was calculated for each animal class based on the composition of the feed ration and average values for crude protein content of individual feeds. Average crude pro- tein content values were taken from Feedpedia (2015).
Indirect N20 emissions related to volatilization and leaching pro- cesses of manure N management were also assessed based on IPCC
Tier 2 approach considering nitrogen excreted and manure manage- ment system.
Factors for estimation of emissions from manure management are summarized in Table 4.

39: The carbon footprint assessment was carried out according to the model and methodology developed by the IPCC (IPCC, 2013). Current IPCC 100-year global warming potential (GWP) characterization factors were applied to convert greenhouse gas emissions into carbon dioxide equivalent (CO2eq) emissions: all the GHGs aggregate into one indicator that is CO2ed The characterization factors used were 1, 28, 25.25 and 265 for carbon dioxide, fossil methane, biogenic methane (for enteric fer- mentation emissions) and nitrous oxide, respectively.
40: The results of total carbon footprint of the two farms (Table 5) sug- gest that ORG beef production system in Umbria region (24.62 kg CO2eq/ kg live weight) is more greenhouse gas intensive than CON system (18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight).

For CON systems, other authors (Pelletier et al., 2010; Alig et al., 2012) obtained similar values of CF, ranging from 15.3 to 16.2 kg CO2eq/kg live weight; a comparison with an Italian EPD on beef meat, considering the same breeding stages (Environdec, 2013), provides an higher value (20.8 kg CO2eq/kg boneless meat), but several differences on the production chain are evident.

In both scenarios, the main contribution to the whole CF value is re- lated to cow-calf stage, about 60% for CON farm and 63% for ORG one of total emissions; this value is in accordance with an other study that pro- vides a value of 63%, based on the same functional unit (Pelletier et al., 2010).

Studies on ORG beef production are less common in the Literature and the results are strongly dependent on cattle breed, finishing age, and type of diet. Meier et al. (2015), basing on 3 studies on beef
Fig. 2. CF of feed production (kg CO2eq/f.u.) with different contributions of livestock category in the two farms.
production, showed that ORG systems not always give better environ- mental performance than conventional systems.

Despite the good environmental behavior of the feed production phase in the ORG farm, thanks to the free - synthetic fertilization, the whole value of CF is worst due to the longer finishing period (24 months than 20 of the CON farm) that increases the emissions from enteric fer- mentation and manure management.
The energy used in the animal house causes the same greenhouse gas emissions in the two farms because it is only related to animals feed- ing and cattle house cleaning.
The positive contribution of nitrogen avoided emissions from ma- nure disposed to nearby farms accounts for 1% of total CF value.
Detailed discussions on CF of different subsystems of the beef pro- duction chains are given in the following.

43: The results of the carbon footprint of the feed production for the two farms are reported in Fig. 2. The contributions are expressed for differ- ent livestock categories in the farms and referred to the functional unit.

The major contribution to CF of feed production derived from cow- calf stage, according to other studies that demonstrate the CF in both beef production systems is strongly related to the maintaining of the COWS and bulls (De Vries et al., 2015).

The ORG carbon footprint in the feed production is lower than CON farm, due to partially reduction of carbon dioxide and nitrous oxide emissions related to the absence of synthetic fertilization practice.

In Table 6 are reported the percentage impacts on CF of feed produc- tion from different type of feed produced in the CON and ORG farms; forage includes ryegrass for CON farm and alfalfa for ORG one while con- centrates represent cereal meals (maize, barley, faba bean in ORG diet and sorghum and triticale in the CON one).
In the CON system the 71% of emissions from feed production is re- lated to forage cultivation while for the ORG farm the 72% is caused by cereals growing and subsequent milling.

45: The environmental evaluation of beef production phase is essentially related to the enteric fermentation of cattles, that causes CH4 emissions in the atmosphere.

According to other authors (Meier et al., 2015), enteric methane is the main key contributor to life cycle of the two cattle livestock farms, respectively with 50% and 55% of total emissions for CON and ORG farm.

The CH4 rate is highly dependent on type of diet of the animals and feed digestibility (Rearte and Pordomingo, 2014): a roughage-based feeding produce more CH4 emissions than a concentrate-based diet (Casey and Holden, 2006a, 2006b; Nguyen et al., 2010).

In the CON farm, type of diet of cows and bulls is different from the ORG farm: the first is characterized by high amounts of ryegrass with 27.5% of fiber and some concentrate feeds as sorghum, maize, and triti- cale meals, while the second is essentially a roughage-based system with grass-product (alfalfa hay) and non-grass roughage feed as maize silage and barley straw (Tables 2 and 3).

This second type of feeding causes a greater amount of enteric emis- sion for ORG farm, especially derived from cows and bulls digestion (cow-calf phase), as shown in Table 7; this contribution is almost dou- ble than the CON farm. Coderoni et al. (2015) confirm that in Italy the enteric emissions of COWS contribute almost 40% to the total CF of beef.
In addition, in the ORG farm, the average digestibility of feeds is higher than in the CON farm, due to the use of straw and maize silage which are difficult to digest for ruminant apparatus.

Furthermore, Hristov et al. (2013) concluded that inclusion of con- centrate feeds in the diet of ruminants will likely decrease enteric meth- ane, particularly when inclusions are above 35% to 40% of DMI; in the

Coderoni et al. (2015) confirm that in Italy the enteric emissions of COWS contribute almost 40% to the total CF of beef.

Furthermore, Hristov et al. (2013) concluded that inclusion of con- centrate feeds in the diet of ruminants will likely decrease enteric meth- ane, particularly when inclusions are above 35% to 40% of DMI; in the
47: Environmental impact (above 2%) on CF of feed production subsystem of different diet cat- egory for the CON and ORG farm.
two farm cases, the concentrates amount in the cows diet is 27% for CON farm and 21% for ORG farm.

Small amounts of concentrate feeds will increase animal productiv- ity and thus decrease GHG but, if emissions from concentrate feed pro- duction are included, absolute GHG emissions may not always decrease (Opio et al., 2013).
48: As already described for enteric emissions, due to essentially rough- age based diet, higher CH4 emissions from manure storage are attribut- ed to ORG system.
Results show that the major contribution to CF of beef production from manure storage is really caused by CH4 source and, to a lesser ex- tent, by nitrogen emissions.

In addition, Hindrichsen et al. (2006) showed that CH4 emissions from slurry increased when cows were fed mixed forage-concentrate diets instead of forage-only diets; this confirms the more intensive greenhouse gases emissions nature of the ORG animals diet, while CON farm is characterized by a diet based mainly on forage feeding (as already shown in Table 1).
In Table 8, the N-flows in the two different farms are reported for all the livestock categories.

Feed composition on CON and ORG farms affected also nitrogen flows deriving from manure storage; in particular, the crude protein content influences the nitrogen intake for the cattles and so the excreted amounts (IPCC, 2006).

As consequence of different diet regime and N-excretion rates, higher emissions of N compounds from ORG farm are obtained for heifers and bullocks during fattening and finishing steps; a smaller gross energy value for bulls diet, instead, involves a less amount of nitro- gen emissions from manure for these livestock category with respect to CON farm.

50: In the Literature the adding of carbon sequestration by soils in the feed production is a highly debated topic (Mogensen et al., 2014) in ag- ricultural practices, such as organic farming. However the estimation of this kind of contribution to the carbon footprint of beef production is ac- tually very unreliable due to the small amount of certain data (de Léis et al., 2015). An interesting approach used in this study about the inclusion of soil carbon changes in the Life Cycle Assessment of agricultural prod- ucts was firstly developed by Petersen et al. (2013) and then employed by Mogensen et al. (2014) in order to calculate the carbon sequestration of cattle feeds. It is based on a single year's addition of carbon from dif- ferent sources, such as manure and crop residues, and the related effect on the CO2 emissions in the atmosphere. In particular, this approach al- lows to evaluate differences in carbon sequestration between crops. In this study durum wheat with no straw removed and no manure input was assumed as the reference crop with a carbon sequestration equal

|CON|CON|CON|CON|ORG|ORG|ORG|ORG |Heifers and bullocks (5-18 months)|Finishing heifers and bullocks (18-20 months)|Cows|Bulls|Heifers and bullocks (5-18 months)|Finishing heifers and bullocks (18-24 months)|Cows|Bulls Crude protein (% DMI)|9.6|10.1|8.8|8.7|10.6|12.9|9.2|9.2 N intake (kg N/cattle * year)|45.0|82.3|68.5|89.2|47.7|87.7|84.7|84.7 N retention (kg N/cattle year)|10.4|13.8|6.2|2.9|12.4|16.3|7.6|2.6 N excretion (kg N/cattle year)|34.6|68.5|62.3|86.3|35.3|71.4|77.1|82.1 CF from nitrogen emission (kg CO2eq/f.u.)|0.3|0.1|0.8|0.8|0.4|0.2|0.7|0.7
to 0 kg C/ha. The choice was motivated by the diffusion of the crop and
this kind of farming technique in Italy. In fact, durum wheat is the main
cereal cultivated in Italy (Diotallevi et al., 2015) and straw after harvest-
ing is mainly left on the soil (Monteleone et al., 2015). Furthermore, the
organic farming is not widespread in Italy, covering only about 8% of the
total agricultural land (Mazzoncini et al., 2015). Petersen et al. (2013)
assumed that 10% of the carbon input into soils is sequestered in a
100-year perspective. Carbon input was considered as the sum of
above-ground (AG) biomass, below-ground (BG) crop residues and ma-
nure. The carbon content of AG and BG biomass was assumed as 45% of
the dry matter while the carbon input of the manure was determined
assuming a C:N ratio of 21:1 (Mogensen et al., 2014). A land use factor
equal to 0.8 for all crops (IPCC, 2013) was considered taking into ac-
count the root turnover and the duration of the crops. In addition the ef-
fect of tillage on the carbon sequestration was considered assuming a
factor of 1 for all crops (IPCC, 2013). According to this approach, crops
with a higher carbon input than durum wheat would result in carbon
sequestration over time and crops with a lower carbon input than
durum wheat would result in a carbon emission over time.
The effective carbon input into the soil of each crop was calculated
by subtracting 2609 kg C/ha, related to durum wheat (Table 9).
For both scenarios, carbon sequestration allows to reduce the carbon
footprint of beef, mainly due to the employment of high amounts of ma-
nure produced as deep bedding. However its contribution on the CF of
beef is limited for both production systems, allowing to decrease the
GHG emissions from 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight (Table 5) to 23.32
kg CO2eq/kg live weight and from 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight (Table 5) to 17.71 kg CO2eq/kg live weight, for CON and ORG farms respectively.

As highlighted in other studies, carbon sequestration by the soils is characterized by a large range of variation (Picasso et al., 2014; Jones and Donnelly, 2004) and it has a major impact on the GHG balance when the beef production is based on grazing (Soussana et al., 2010); in this case livestock systems may become net sinks of GHG.
However according to Soussana et al. (2010) the evaluation of the carbon stock changes requires long-term experiments and major inter- national collaborative effort.

equal to 0.8 for all crops (IPCC, 2013) was considered taking into ac-
count the root turnover and the duration of the crops. In addition the ef-
fect of tillage on the carbon sequestration was considered assuming a
factor of 1 for all crops (IPCC, 2013). According to this approach, crops
with a higher carbon input than durum wheat would result in carbon
sequestration over time and crops with a lower carbon input than
durum wheat would result in a carbon emission over time.
The effective carbon input into the soil of each crop was calculated
by subtracting 2609 kg C/ha, related to durum wheat (Table 9).
For both scenarios, carbon sequestration allows to reduce the carbon
footprint of beef, mainly due to the employment of high amounts of ma-
nure produced as deep bedding. However its contribution on the CF of
beef is limited for both production systems, allowing to decrease the
GHG emissions from 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight (Table 5) to 23.32
kg CO2eq/kg live weight and from 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight (Table 5) to 17.71 kg CO2eq/kg live weight, for CON and ORG farms respectively.

As highlighted in other studies, carbon sequestration by the soils is characterized by a large range of variation (Picasso et al., 2014; Jones and Donnelly, 2004) and it has a major impact on the GHG balance when the beef production is based on grazing (Soussana et al., 2010); in this case livestock systems may become net sinks of GHG.
However according to Soussana et al. (2010) the evaluation of the carbon stock changes requires long-term experiments and major inter- national collaborative effort.

to 0 kg C/ha. The choice was motivated by the diffusion of the crop and
this kind of farming technique in Italy. In fact, durum wheat is the main
cereal cultivated in Italy (Diotallevi et al., 2015) and straw after harvest-
ing is mainly left on the soil (Monteleone et al., 2015). Furthermore, the
organic farming is not widespread in Italy, covering only about 8% of the
total agricultural land (Mazzoncini et al., 2015). Petersen et al. (2013)
assumed that 10% of the carbon input into soils is sequestered in a
100-year perspective. Carbon input was considered as the sum of
above-ground (AG) biomass, below-ground (BG) crop residues and ma-
nure. The carbon content of AG and BG biomass was assumed as 45% of
the dry matter while the carbon input of the manure was determined
assuming a C:N ratio of 21:1 (Mogensen et al., 2014). A land use factor
equal to 0.8 for all crops (IPCC, 2013) was considered taking into ac-
count the root turnover and the duration of the crops. In addition the ef-
fect of tillage on the carbon sequestration was considered assuming a
factor of 1 for all crops (IPCC, 2013). According to this approach, crops
with a higher carbon input than durum wheat would result in carbon
sequestration over time and crops with a lower carbon input than
durum wheat would result in a carbon emission over time.
The effective carbon input into the soil of each crop was calculated
by subtracting 2609 kg C/ha, related to durum wheat (Table 9).
For both scenarios, carbon sequestration allows to reduce the carbon
footprint of beef, mainly due to the employment of high amounts of ma-
nure produced as deep bedding. However its contribution on the CF of
beef is limited for both production systems, allowing to decrease the
GHG emissions from 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight (Table 5) to 23.32
kg CO2eq/kg live weight and from 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight (Table 5) to 17.71 kg CO2eq/kg live weight, for CON and ORG farms respectively.

As highlighted in other studies, carbon sequestration by the soils is characterized by a large range of variation (Picasso et al., 2014; Jones and Donnelly, 2004) and it has a major impact on the GHG balance when the beef production is based on grazing (Soussana et al., 2010); in this case livestock systems may become net sinks of GHG.
However according to Soussana et al. (2010) the evaluation of the carbon stock changes requires long-term experiments and major inter- national collaborative effort.
55: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 56: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 57: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 58: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

manure storage (Powell et al., 2008). Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. 59: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 60: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during
Table 9 Annual crop residues and C sequestration per ha.
b	For faba bean above-ground and below-ground biomass was estimated according to IPCC (2006).
C Losses left in field (% of crop yield): 1% for durum wheat, sorghum, maize, triticale and barley; 14% for ryegrass and alfalfa; 7% for maize silage (Mogensen et al., 2014).
d	For durum wheat straw yield was assumed as 55% of the crop yield (Mogensen et al., 2014). For the other crops straw is removed from the field.
f Obtained multiplying Total C input' for the land use and tillage factors.
g From C to CO2 by multiplication with 44:12 (negative value means C sequestration while positive number means C loss).

manure storage (Powell et al., 2008). However, the adoption of these diets should be evaluated considering the economic viability and the ef- fects on the animal health.|Cerri, C.C., Silva Moreira, C., Alves, P.A., Raucci, G.S., Castigioni, B.d.A., Mello, F.F.C., Cerri, D.G.P., Cerri, C.E.P., 2016. Climate change mitigation options in the Italian livestock sector. EuroChoices 14 (1), 17-24. and methanogens for CH4 (Cerri et al., 2016). In particular, methane emissions from the storage of solid manure can potentially be reduced|de Léis, C.M., Cherubini, E., Ruviaro, C.F., Prudêncio da Silva, V., do Nascimento Lampert, V., Spies, A., Soares, S.R., 2015. Carbon footprint of milk production in Brazil: a compar- ative case study. Int. J. Life Cyc. Assess. 20, 46-60. by promoting or avoiding anaerobic conditions. Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 61: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 62: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 63: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 64: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 65: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 66: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during
