Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 67: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 68: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 69: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

manure storage (Powell et al., 2008). Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 70: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 71: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during
Table 9 Annual crop residues and C sequestration per ha.
b	For faba bean above-ground and below-ground biomass was estimated according to IPCC (2006).
C Losses left in field (% of crop yield): 1% for durum wheat, sorghum, maize, triticale and barley; 14% for ryegrass and alfalfa; 7% for maize silage (Mogensen et al., 2014).
d	For durum wheat straw yield was assumed as 55% of the crop yield (Mogensen et al., 2014). For the other crops straw is removed from the field.
f Obtained multiplying Total C input' for the land use and tillage factors.
g From C to CO2 by multiplication with 44:12 (negative value means C sequestration while positive number means C loss).

manure storage (Powell et al., 2008). Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 72: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 73: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013. 74: Several reduction options are available in the Literature in order to reduce the carbon footprint of beef production systems. The methane emissions from the enteric fermentation, which were identified as the largest source of GHG emissions in this study, could be reduced by mod- ifying the dietary composition and increasing the feed conversion effi- ciency (Gollnow et al., 2014). To this aim, several strategies were tested using dietary supplements, including tannins and saponins or forage additives, such as ionophores and defaunation (Luo et al., 2015; Williams et al., 2009); the use of chemical additives can modify rumen microbial fermentation, obtaining a reduction of methane emission by about 30% (Luo et al., 2015). Furthermore, the optimization of the N content of the diet, by reducing the crude protein content, could de- crease N excretion and therefore nitrous oxide emissions during

Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 of 28 June 2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products and Repealing Regulation (EEC) No 2092/91. Euro- pean Commission, Brussels. "The beef production is particularly relevant for the Italian agro-in- dustrial sector and is one of the main sources of GHG emissions. The present work investigates the CF of beef production in two representa- tive organic and conventional farms in Italy by a Life Cycle approach, in order to analyze the environmental impacts in the all stages of produc- tion from ""cradle to gate"", and suggests possible mitigation strategies for farmers. The results show that the carbon footprint of organic and conven- tional beef are quite different: the environmental impact of ORG chain is 24.62 kg CO2eq/kg live weight against 18.21 kg CO2eq/kg live weight of CON system. The gap is related to the higher amount of methane emissions derived from enteric fermentation and manure management. In ORG farm, the longer finishing period and type of diet caused the higher enteric fermentation and manure management emissions, de- spite the advantage of the absence of synthetic fertilizing that allows a low impact for feed production. The contribution to the soil carbon sequestration caused a small de- crease of GHG emissions for both production systems. Nevertheless, the results obtained in this study cannot be extended to other Italian regions without appropriate evaluations about climate conditions, livestock management, and animal productivity characteris- tics. Future improvements could regard the application of the mitigation strategies to reduce the GHG emissions of the beef production chain; in particular, the effects of different type of feed and the application of ma- nure treatments could be investigated."|"EC, 2013.
